note: Popper (Huang et al. 2025, arXiv:2502.09858) is an agentic sequential-falsification framework for validating free-form hypotheses. Core mechanism: iterative design->execute->aggregate loop; Experiment Design Agent proposes falsification sub-hypotheses (null h0i / alt h1i), ReAct Execution Agent returns a p-value each round. Strict Type-I error control at alpha=0.1 via p-to-e calibrator (ei = kappa * pi^(kappa-1)), e-values aggregated multiplicatively into a super-martingale/e-process with any-time validity (Theorem 4: P(y-hat=1) <= alpha under H0). Full-text grep returned ZERO hits for 'information gain', 'EIG', 'expected information', 'mutual information', 'entropy', 'acquisition function'. Experiment selection is driven by implication strength + LLM-as-judge relevance checker + novelty/implementability, NOT any information-theoretic objective. Manuscript claim C114 therefore verified on all three prongs.

C114.claim_text: Popper runs sequential falsification experiments with statistical error control (huang2025popper) -- but neither computes or maximizes EIG: they substitute a falsification heuristic for the BED-optimal policy rather than realizing it.
evidence: (a) SEQUENTIAL FALSIFICATION EXPERIMENTS -- CONFIRMED. Sec 2.2: 'POPPER is an agentic framework to systematically validate a hypothesis by actively designing and executing a sequence of falsification experiments.' Sec 2.4 / Algorithm 1 (App A.1): at each round i, Design Agent proposes a falsification test for sub-hypothesis h0i, Execution Agent (ReAct, Yao et al. 2023) carries it out and reports p-value pi; loop repeats over iterations accumulating evidence.

(b) STATISTICAL ERROR CONTROL -- CONFIRMED. Sec 2.3: primary criterion is classical Type-I error control at pre-defined level alpha in (0,1); Type-I rate = sup_{P in P0} P(y-hat=1). Sec 3 Eq.(1): p-to-e calibrator ei = kappa * pi^(kappa-1); aggregated Ei = prod_{s<=i} es; reject H0 if Ei >= 1/alpha. Theorem 4 (proof App A.2): under Assumptions 1-3, E is a valid e-value and P(y-hat=1) <= alpha under H0. Sec 4.1: nominal alpha = 0.1; Table 3 shows POPPER Type-I 0.103/0.082/0.085 (DiscoveryBench/IL2/IFNG), all at/below nominal.

(c) NEITHER COMPUTES NOR MAXIMIZES EIG -- CONFIRMED. Full-text search for 'information gain', 'EIG', 'expected information', 'mutual information', 'entropy', 'acquisition function' returned 0 matches. The paper's optimization target for experiment selection is implication strength/relevance: Design Agent (System Prompt, Listing 3) is prompted to 'maximize the implication strength of the proposed falsification test'; a Relevance Checker R(h) in [0.1,1.0] (LLM-as-a-judge, Sec 3) prunes low-relevance tests; Self-Refinement (Madaan et al. 2024) optimizes novelty/implementability/logical-relevance. No Bayesian experimental design, no information-theoretic acquisition anywhere in the paper. So Popper substitutes a falsification/relevance heuristic for any EIG/BED-optimal objective -- exactly as the manuscript states.
C114.fix: None needed. Claim is accurate on all three prongs (sequential falsification, Type-I statistical error control, EIG absent). Optional precision (not required): 'statistical error control' in Popper is specifically Type-I error control at alpha=0.1 via e-values/e-processes; FDR/FWER are only sketched as future work (App C), not implemented.

reverse_findings.fact: Popper's error control is specifically Type-I error control (alpha=0.1) realized through e-values / e-processes (super-martingale, any-time valid, optional stopping via Theorem 4), converting each per-experiment p-value with the p-to-e calibrator ei = kappa * pi^(kappa-1). FDR and FWER control are explicitly future work (App C), NOT implemented.
reverse_findings.loc: Sec 2.3, Sec 3 Eq.(1), Theorem 4 / App A.2; App C
reverse_findings.mismatch: No mismatch with C114 as worded ('statistical error control' is a fair umbrella for Type-I control). Flag only so the manuscript does not elsewhere over-generalize Popper's guarantee to FDR/FWER-style control, which the paper disclaims.

reverse_findings.fact: Popper does not frame itself as a Bayesian experimental design (BED) method or as approximating a BED-optimal policy; it is presented purely as a sequential hypothesis-testing / Popperian-falsification framework. The 'BED-optimal policy' contrast is the manuscript's own interpretive framing, not a claim made by Popper.
reverse_findings.loc: Sec 2.2-2.4 (framework description); Sec 5 / App B (related work positions it against hypothesis-generation and code-generation workflows, never BED)
reverse_findings.mismatch: No factual error. The manuscript correctly characterizes Popper as substituting a falsification heuristic 'rather than realizing' the BED-optimal policy -- consistent with Popper never invoking EIG/BED. Noted for transparency: the BED framing originates with the citing manuscript, and Popper's experiment-selection objective is implication strength/relevance, not an approximate EIG surrogate.

reverse_findings.fact: Experiment selection in Popper is guided by three heuristic criteria via Self-Refinement (novelty, implementability, logical relevance) plus an LLM-as-a-judge Relevance Checker; the Design Agent is explicitly told to 'maximize the implication strength' of the proposed test. This is the substituted heuristic referenced in C114.
reverse_findings.loc: Sec 3 (Instantiation of the experiment design agent; Relevance checker); Listing 3 (Statistical Agent system prompt); Listing 4 (Relevance Checker prompt)
reverse_findings.mismatch: Supports and sharpens C114 -- confirms a non-EIG, relevance/implication-strength heuristic is what drives test choice.
